title: Designing a URL Shorting service like TinyURL
description: Designing a URL Shorting service like TinyURL
requirements: Base62 Encoding vs. Key Generation Service (KGS)
key_topics: Base62 Encoding vs. Key Generation Service (KGS)
theory: URL shortening creates shorter aliases for long URLs to save space. We can use a hash function on the original URL and encode it to Base62. Alternatively, generate random strings offline using a Key Generation Service.
bottlenecks: Database read load can be a bottleneck.
Adding a caching layer (LRU) helps.
Single point of failure in KGS.
diagramHints: Draw clients connecting to a Load Balancer, which routes to Web Servers. Web servers read from an in-memory Cache and a NoSQL Database. Show a background Key Generation Service populating the database with unused keys.

title: Designing Pastebin
theory: Pastebin allows users to store plain text. It requires generating a unique URL and storing potentially large text blocks. A KGS can be used to generate keys.
bottlenecks: Handling massive read requests requires caching.
Writing large text blocks limits concurrent connections.
diagramHints: Show a split between Read Servers and Write Servers. Write servers talk to Object Storage for payloads and a Metadata DB for the URL mapping.

title: Designing Instagram
requirements: News Feed Generation (Pull vs. Push vs. Hybrid)
key_topics: News Feed Generation (Pull vs. Push vs. Hybrid)
theory: Instagram is a read-heavy system. It stores user metadata, social graphs, and photos. Newsfeed is generated offline to ensure low latency.
bottlenecks: Generating newsfeeds synchronously is too slow.
The social graph causes hotspots.
diagramHints: Draw Image Storage, Metadata Storage, and a dedicated Newsfeed Generation Service. Show users uploading via Write API and pulling feeds via Read API.

title: Designing Dropbox
requirements: Client architecture (Watcher, Chunker, Indexer, Internal DB)
key_topics: Client architecture (Watcher, Chunker, Indexer, Internal DB)
theory: Cloud storage syncs files between devices. We break files into smaller chunks. When a file is updated, only the modified chunk is synchronized.
bottlenecks: Syncing chunks concurrently requires robust conflict resolution.
Bandwidth is bottlenecked without chunking and deduplication.
diagramHints: Draw the Chunker on the client side. The client talks to Block Servers directly for data uploading, and to Metadata Servers for syncing file structure.

title: Designing Facebook Messenger
requirements: Push model using WebSockets / Long Polling
key_topics: Push model using WebSockets / Long Polling
theory: Chat systems require full-duplex communication for real-time delivery. Messages are stored persistently using a sequence number per user for ordering.
bottlenecks: Managing millions of concurrent open HTTP connections.
Presence service state changes cause huge fanout traffic.
diagramHints: Show users connected to Chat Servers with persistent WebSockets. Chat Servers talk to a Presence DB and write to a massive NoSQL Database concurrently.

title: Designing Twitter
theory: Twitter is heavily read-biased. It revolves around a publish/subscribe fanout model. When a user posts, it 'fans out' to followers' timelines.
bottlenecks: Celebrity fanout can cause extreme latency.
Using mixed models fixes this.
diagramHints: Draw standard users publishing to a Fanout Service which pushes updates to Redis caches of followers. Show celebrity tweets being kept in a separate global Cache.

title: Designing Youtube or Netflix
theory: Video streaming involves video ingestion, encoding into multiple formats, and streaming distribution via Content Delivery Networks (CDN).
bottlenecks: Serving videos directly from origin servers would crush bandwidth.
Encoding videos synchronously slows down availability.
diagramHints: Show Video Upload flow into a Processing Queue where Worker Nodes perform Encoding. Encoded videos are pushed to a CDN.

title: Designing Typeahead Suggestion
requirements: Storing top suggestions at each node
key_topics: Storing top suggestions at each node
theory: Typeahead implies real-time completion. Trie data structures are optimal for prefix-matching at high scale.
bottlenecks: Updating the Trie in real-time is expensive. Updates should be done in background batches.
Storing large Tries requires sharding.
diagramHints: Draw users typing into an API which queries a 'Trie Cache'. Show backend MapReduce workers processing logs to update the global Trie.

title: Designing an API Rate Limiter
requirements: Fixed Window vs. Rolling Window Algorithms
key_topics: Fixed Window vs. Rolling Window Algorithms
theory: Rate Limiting restricts traffic to protect APIs from abuse. Common algorithms include Fixed Window, Sliding Window Log, and Sliding Window Counters (most optimal memory).
bottlenecks: Race conditions in Redis when counting concurrently.
Latency overhead of checking limits for every API call.
diagramHints: Clients hit an API Gateway, which queries a Rate Limiter component. The Rate Limiter reads counters from Redis. If allowed, it forwards to Backend App Servers.

title: Designing a Web Crawler
theory: Web crawling requires picking a seeds URL, fetching HTML, extracting links, and resolving duplicates efficiently.
bottlenecks: DNS resolution is extremely slow unless aggressively cached.
Politeness rules prevent spamming a single domain.
diagramHints: Draw a core loop: URL Frontier -> HTML Fetcher -> Content Seen Check -> Link Extractor -> URL Seen Check -> Back to URL Frontier.

title: Designing Facebook's Newsfeed
theory: A newsfeed compiles updates from friends, pages, and groups. Offline generation provides sub-second latency.
bottlenecks: Ranking algorithms are intensive.
Huge datasets make real-time compilation impossible.
diagramHints: Show a robust Fanout-on-write process updating follower cache lists. Display a fast Fanout-on-load process fetching data directly from cache.

title: Designing Yelp or Nearby Friends
theory: Location-based services use spatial indexing techniques like Geohash or QuadTrees to quickly find points of interest nearby.
bottlenecks: Updating QuadTrees is complex if users are constantly moving (e.g. Uber). Yelp places are static, so it's easier.
Extreme read-heavy loads for queries.
diagramHints: Draw an LBS (Location Based Service) connecting to an in-memory QuadTree cluster, which occasionally syncs to a persistent spatial database.
